lesson.title: Modulo in practice: parity, cycles, positions
masteryOutcomes: Use x % 2 to detect parity and explain why it works.
Apply modulo to cyclic positions (e.g., every Nth item, rotating shifts) to simplify branching.

concept: Using % as a pattern tool: x % 2 for even/odd, and x % n to wrap endless counts onto repeating cycles like weekdays and rotating shifts
prerequisites: Division modes: // and % giving quotient and remainder, and the rebuild identity a == b * (a // b) + a % b

explanations.text: Here's the trick: % 2 sorts every whole number into two teams. Even numbers split into pairs with nobody left over, so x % 2 gives 0. Odd numbers always have one loner, so x % 2 gives 1. That's a built-in even/odd detector. And % is bigger than 2: it's how you count on anything that repeats. A week repeats every 7 days, so 'what day is it 10 days after day 2?' is (2 + 10) % 7 — the full weeks vanish and you land on 5. Think of a clock face: % takes a huge count, walks it around the circle, and tells you where your finger stops.

x % 2 is the parity test: it asks 'what's left after removing all the pairs?' Even numbers leave 0, odd numbers leave 1, and those are the only possible answers — print(10 % 2) shows 0, print(7 % 2) shows 1. You can even print the test itself: print(10 % 2 == 0) shows True. The same idea scales to any cycle of length n: x % n converts an ever-growing count into a position from 0 to n - 1 that repeats forever. Three flatmates rotating dinner duty? Dinner number 17 belongs to position 17 % 3, which is 2. Today is weekday 2 and you want 10 days ahead? (2 + 10) % 7 is 5. One detail that trips people: when the count is smaller than the cycle, % just returns it — 3 % 7 is 3, because after zero full weeks you're still on day 3.

Why parity works: by the identity from last lesson, every integer is (x // 2) complete pairs plus x % 2 left over. With divisor 2 the remainder can only be 0 or 1, and it is exactly the 'is there a loner?' bit — that bit is parity. (A Python bonus: it survives negatives, because % with a positive divisor never goes negative — -3 % 2 is 1, correctly odd.) The general pattern: any process that repeats with period n is fully described by its position in 0..n - 1, and x % n is the projection onto that circle. Full cycles vanish because they're exact multiples of n — 14 % 7 is 0, meaning two complete weeks land you back at the start — and the result can never reach n itself. This is why % simplifies branching: instead of an endless ladder of cases ('if the count is 3, or 6, or 9...'), one expression — count % 3 == 0 — captures the whole infinite family, and one formula — (start + steps) % n — replaces all wraparound bookkeeping, whether steps is 10 or 10000.

hintLadder.text: Find what repeats in the problem and count how long one full cycle is — that length is your divisor.
Work out the total count of steps from the start; the cycle length only enters at the very end.
Full cycles don't change where you stand — % is the operator that throws them away and keeps only your position.
Test your formula twice: once with a count smaller than the cycle, once with a bigger one, and walk each on your fingers to compare.

misconceptions.trigger: Flips the parity results — claims x % 2 == 1 means even
misconceptions.correction: Remember what % 2 measures: the loner left after pairing. Evens pair perfectly (remainder 0); odds leave one behind (remainder 1). Run print(10 % 2) and print(7 % 2) and match each output to its word.

misconceptions.trigger: Reads % as percent, expecting something like 50 % 10 to compute a percentage
misconceptions.correction: In Python, % is the remainder operator — nothing to do with percentages. 50 % 10 is 0, because 10 fits 5 whole times into 50 with nothing left over.

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks 3 % 7 must be an error, or 7, because 3 is smaller than 7
misconceptions.correction: 3 % 7 is just 3: seven fits zero whole times, so all 3 remain. A count smaller than the cycle is already its own position — no error, no wraparound needed yet.

misconceptions.trigger: Expects 14 % 7 to be 7 after two full weeks
misconceptions.correction: A remainder can never reach the divisor — % 7 only ever returns 0 through 6. Two exact weeks means you're back at the start: 14 % 7 is 0.

misconceptions.trigger: Handles wraparound by hand, subtracting the cycle length repeatedly (12 - 7 = 5) instead of using %
misconceptions.correction: Your subtraction is exactly what % does — done any number of times at once. (2 + 10) % 7 is 5 in one step, and the same formula still works 1000 days ahead, where manual subtracting falls apart.

misconceptions.trigger: Confidently claims you need a separate rule for each case — 'every 3rd item, so check 3, then 6, then 9...'
misconceptions.correction: That ladder never ends. count % 3 == 0 captures every multiple of 3 in one expression — test it: print(9 % 3 == 0) shows True and print(10 % 3 == 0) shows False.

checks.prompt:
Predict both outputs, then explain in one sentence why % 2 detects even and odd:
print(24 % 2)
print(13 % 2)
acceptanceCriteria: Explains that % 2 is the leftover after grouping into pairs
States that 0 and 1 are the only possible results of % 2, mapping 0 to even and 1 to odd
checks.passFeedback: Exactly — pairs vanish, the loner shows up as 1, and there are only two possible verdicts. That's parity, fully explained.
checks.failFeedback: Picture the number as pairs of socks: 24 pairs up perfectly (remainder 0), 13 leaves one sock alone (remainder 1). Run both lines and match the outputs to even and odd.

checks.prompt: Three flatmates rotate dinner duty: turn 0 is Ana, turn 1 is Ben, turn 2 is Caro, then it repeats. Starting from dinner = 17, use % to print which turn number (0, 1, or 2) cooks dinner number 17, so the output is exactly: Cook: 2
acceptanceCriteria: Uses dinner % 3 to compute the position
checks.passFeedback: Five full rotations vanish and position 2 remains — Caro cooks. You just replaced a pile of case-checking with one expression.
checks.failFeedback: The rotation is 3 turns long, so the divisor is 3. Ask % to discard the complete rotations in 17 and keep your position: walk 17 on your fingers around Ana-Ben-Caro to verify.

practice.prompt: A playlist of 6 songs repeats forever; positions are 0 through 5, and play number 0 is position 0. Starting from songs = 6 and plays = 100, print the position playing at play number 100, so the output is exactly: Position: 4
acceptanceCriteria: Uses plays % songs to compute the position

practice.prompt: Explain why x % 2 works as an even/odd detector, then generalize: what does x % n tell you about any repeating cycle of length n?
rubric: % 2 returns the leftover after removing pairs: 0 for even, 1 for odd — the only two possible remainders
Generalization: x % n gives the position within the current cycle, in the range 0 to n - 1, because complete cycles are exact multiples of n and vanish
Mentions a concrete cycle example, such as weekdays with % 7 or rotating turns with % 3

practice.prompt: Today is weekday 5 (with weekday 0 meaning Monday). What does print((5 + 9) % 7) show?
practice.explanation: 5 + 9 is 14, and 14 % 7 is 0 — fourteen is exactly two full weeks, so you land back on weekday 0, Monday. 7 and 14 are impossible outputs: % 7 only ever returns 0 through 6.

pedagogyRules: Have the student walk each new modulo formula once by hand — on fingers or a drawn circle — predict the result, then run and compare, before they're allowed to trust it.

If the student asks a jump-ahead question (if statements, loops, lists), answer in one sentence — e.g. 'if turns these 0-or-1 results into decisions, coming soon' — and return to the current cycle problem.
If the student says they already know modulo, verify lightly: ask for 3 % 7, 14 % 7, and 13 % 2 with reasons; three exact answers means jump straight to the dinner-rotation check, no re-drill.
If the student is lost, drop cycles entirely and rebuild from pairing: have them compute 6 % 2, 7 % 2, 8 % 2, 9 % 2 in a row and say the 0-1-0-1 pattern aloud before any clock or weekday talk.

When the student is wrong but confident (e.g. insists 14 % 7 is 7, or that 3 % 7 errors), don't argue — run the single line together, read the output, and restate the 0-to-n-minus-1 range rule it demonstrates.
If the student wants to skip ahead, allow it after a first-try pass on the dinner-rotation check; otherwise revisit only the specific error they made — flipped parity or an out-of-range remainder.
If frustration appears, acknowledge it, then shrink to the clock-face image — big number in, position on the circle out — and do one tiny example like 10 % 7 together before returning.
If asked why this matters, answer in one line — even/odd checks, alternating row colors, weekday math, and round-robin turn-taking all collapse into a single % expression — then continue.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Check the two classic causes in order: an answer equal to the divisor means the 0-to-n-minus-1 range rule was missed; a flipped even/odd verdict means the 0/1 mapping is reversed. Name the one that actually happened — don't guess at anything else.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Tutor waves through an off-by-one cycle answer
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Verify with the rebuild before confirming: for 17 % 3, five full rotations remove 15 and leave 2 — if the student says 1 or 3, walk the circle with them and show where the count actually lands; never accept 'close enough'.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Scaffolding gap — tutor dumps the finished formula
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Give one hint from the ladder at a time and let the student run code between hints; never hand over (start + steps) % n assembled — the divisor and the total count are theirs to find.
During the parity and rotation checks, never type the expression or say the resulting number first — ask 'how long is one full cycle?' and let the student pick the divisor and produce the line.

chunks.text: x % 2 is the parity test: it returns the leftover after grouping x into pairs. Even numbers pair perfectly, so 10 % 2 is 0; odd numbers leave one over, so 7 % 2 is 1. Those are the only two possible results, so x % 2 == 0 means even and x % 2 == 1 means odd — print(10 % 2 == 0) shows True.

x % n maps any count onto a repeating cycle of length n, returning a position from 0 to n - 1. Full cycles vanish because they're exact multiples of n: 14 % 7 is 0 — two complete weeks land you back at the start — and the result can never equal n itself.

When the count is smaller than the cycle, % returns it unchanged: 3 % 7 is 3, because zero full weeks have passed and you're simply on day 3. No error, no wraparound needed yet — a small count is already its own position.

The cycle formula is position = (start + steps) % n. Worked example — today is weekday 2 and you want 10 days ahead: (2 + 10) % 7 is 5. The same one-line formula works for 10 or 10000 days, because % discards all the completed weeks at once instead of one subtraction at a time.

Rotating turns: with 3 people taking turns, turn number t belongs to position t % 3. Worked example — dinner number 17: 17 % 3 is 2, because 5 full rotations cover 15 dinners and 2 steps remain. 'Every Nth item' is the same idea in reverse: count % 3 == 0 is True exactly on every 3rd count, replacing an endless list of cases with one expression.

celebration.headline: % just became your pattern detector.
celebration.recap: You used x % 2 to split the world into even and odd — and explained why 0 and 1 are the only possible verdicts — then wrapped endless counts onto circles: weekdays with % 7, rotating turns with % 3, one formula instead of a ladder of cases.
celebration.unlocked: Next: rounding and money format — round() and f-strings make your float answers look like real prices.

summaryBullets: You can now use x % 2 to detect parity and explain why it works.
You can now apply modulo to cyclic positions (e.g., every Nth item, rotating shifts) to simplify branching.
You understand using % as a pattern tool: x % 2 for even/odd, and x % n to wrap endless counts onto repeating cycles like weekdays and rotating shifts.
